_meta.authoringNotes: Per Carter & Hudson Best Pract Res Clin Rheumatol 2011 (PMID 21256396), Schmitt Curr Rheumatol Rep 2017 PMID 28238095, and the ASAS classification criteria for axial and peripheral spondyloarthritis (Rudwaleit Ann Rheum Dis 2009 PMID 19297344). Antibiotic data per Carter et al. Arthritis Rheum 2010 (PMID 20662077). HLA-B27 association per Brown et al. Lancet 2002. ACR/EULAR spondyloarthritis recommendations. Selmi & Gershwin Best Pract Res Clin Rheumatol 2014.

h1Title: Reactive Arthritis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Reactive arthritis is a sterile inflammatory arthritis triggered by a gastrointestinal or genitourinary infection elsewhere in the body, classically appearing 1-4 weeks after the inciting infection in a genetically susceptible host. It belongs to the spondyloarthritis family, alongside ankylosing spondylitis and psoriatic arthritis, and is strongly associated with the HLA-B27 allele (present in 30-80% of cases versus 5-10% of the general population). Typical triggers are Chlamydia trachomatis after urogenital infection and Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, or Yersinia after enteric infection; SARS-CoV-2 has been recognized as a more recent trigger. Annual incidence is approximately 0.6-27 cases per 100,000 adults depending on background trigger rates. Presentation features an asymmetric oligoarthritis predominantly of the knees, ankles, and feet, often with enthesitis, dactylitis (sausage digit), low-back pain, conjunctivitis or uveitis, urethritis, and skin lesions (keratoderma blennorrhagicum, circinate balanitis). Most cases resolve within 3-12 months; 15-30% develop chronic disease that requires sustained immunomodulatory care.

keyStats.prevalence: Annual incidence 0.6-27 per 100,000 adults depending on background incidence of triggering infections; lifetime prevalence approximately 30-40 per 100,000
keyStats.demographics: Post-venereal form: male:female ratio approximately 9:1 (related to Chlamydia trachomatis epidemiology). Post-dysenteric form: roughly equal sex distribution. HLA-B27 positive in 30-80% of cases
keyStats.avgAge: Most cases present in young adults aged 20-40 years; uncommon in children but reported after Salmonella, Yersinia, and Campylobacter outbreaks
keyStats.globalCases: True global prevalence under-reported; substantial post-outbreak case clusters documented after foodborne and waterborne enteric infections worldwide

definition: Reactive arthritis (ICD-10: M02; formerly known as Reiter's syndrome and reactive arthropathy) is a sterile inflammatory arthritis that develops 1-4 weeks after a gastrointestinal or urogenital infection. It is classified within the spondyloarthritis family (which also includes ankylosing spondylitis, psoriatic arthritis, and arthritis associated with inflammatory bowel disease) based on shared features: asymmetric peripheral arthritis predominantly affecting lower limbs, enthesitis, dactylitis, axial involvement, association with HLA-B27, and absence of rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. The classical triggering pathogens are Chlamydia trachomatis (urogenital), Salmonella enterica, Shigella flexneri, Campylobacter jejuni, Yersinia enterocolitica, and Clostridioides difficile (gastrointestinal); newer reports add SARS-CoV-2, streptococcal infection, and a range of less common triggers. Pathogenesis is incompletely understood but involves persistence of microbial antigens (and sometimes whole, non-cultivable bacteria) in synovial macrophages, presentation through HLA-B27 to autoreactive T cells, and IL-17/IL-23 axis activation. The historic triad of arthritis, conjunctivitis, and urethritis (formerly called Reiter's syndrome) is present in only 30-50% of patients; modern classification uses the Assessment of SpondyloArthritis (ASAS) criteria for axial or peripheral spondyloarthritis. Disease course ranges from a self-limited monoarthritis or oligoarthritis resolving in 3-12 months to chronic disabling spondyloarthritis indistinguishable from other forms. Care is delivered by rheumatology, often co-managed with infectious diseases, ophthalmology, and dermatology depending on extra-articular manifestations.

typesClassification.description: Triggered by Chlamydia trachomatis urethritis or cervicitis; less commonly Mycoplasma genitalium or Ureaplasma urealyticum. Strong male predominance (9:1) and HLA-B27 association above 70%. Antibiotic treatment of the triggering infection is mandatory for the patient and sexual partners, though it does not reliably reverse established arthritis.

Triggered by Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Yersinia, or Clostridioides difficile gastroenteritis. Roughly equal sex distribution; HLA-B27 positive in 30-60%. Often presents after a recognized outbreak or single severe gastroenteritis episode.

Recognized since 2020 with both acute (during or weeks after COVID-19) and post-acute (months after) presentations. Typical asymmetric lower-limb oligoarthritis with HLA-B27 association in some cohorts. Usually responsive to NSAIDs and short-course corticosteroids.

Post-streptococcal reactive arthritis distinct from acute rheumatic fever; lacks valvular disease, presents with persistent additive or migratory arthritis, and responds to NSAIDs and continued antibiotic prophylaxis where rheumatic fever cannot be excluded.

Persistence of arthritis, enthesitis, or axial disease beyond 6 months affects 15-30% of patients. May evolve into formal axial or peripheral spondyloarthritis requiring long-term DMARD or biologic therapy.

primarySymptoms: Asymmetric oligoarthritis predominantly affecting the lower limbs — most commonly the knees, ankles, midfoot, and toes — appearing 1-4 weeks after the triggering infection.
Dactylitis (sausage digit) — diffuse swelling of an entire finger or toe involving joints, tendons, and entheses, considered a classic spondyloarthritis feature.
Enthesitis with pain at tendon and ligament insertion points, particularly the Achilles tendon, plantar fascia, patellar tendon, and iliac crest — often a prominent and disabling feature.
Inflammatory low-back pain and stiffness from sacroiliitis or spondylitis, especially in patients with HLA-B27 positivity; pain that improves with movement and worsens at rest.
Bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis with redness, foreign-body sensation, and tearing; occasionally progressing to anterior uveitis with photophobia and reduced vision.
Urethritis with dysuria and clear urethral discharge in post-venereal cases (men) or cervicitis (women); urethritis may also occur in post-dysenteric cases without active genital infection.
Keratoderma blennorrhagicum — hyperkeratotic plaques on the soles, palms, and occasionally scalp resembling pustular psoriasis.
Circinate balanitis — painless serpiginous lesions on the glans penis and prepuce in uncircumcised men.
Oral ulcers, particularly on the hard palate and tongue, that are often painless and overlooked.
Constitutional symptoms — fatigue, low-grade fever, weight loss, and malaise during active disease.
earlyWarningSigns: Acute knee, ankle, or foot swelling beginning 1-4 weeks after an episode of urethritis, cervicitis, or gastroenteritis
Dactylitis (sausage finger or toe) without prior trauma or psoriasis
New heel pain and Achilles tendinitis in a young adult after a recent infection
Bilateral red eyes with foreign-body sensation appearing alongside joint symptoms
Inflammatory low-back pain (worse at rest, better with movement, with morning stiffness over 30 minutes) in a young adult after a recent infection
emergencySigns: Acute monoarthritis with fever, severe pain, and inability to bear weight — septic arthritis must be excluded by joint aspiration before assuming reactive arthritis
Sudden eye redness with pain, photophobia, and reduced vision — anterior uveitis requires urgent ophthalmology assessment within 24 hours
Chest pain or new dyspnea — rare aortitis, aortic regurgitation, or conduction system disease in long-standing reactive arthritis
Severe abdominal pain with fever during the prodromal gastroenteritis — exclude bowel complications including toxic megacolon (Shigella, C. difficile)
New numbness, weakness, or bowel/bladder symptoms — exclude cauda equina syndrome in patients with severe axial involvement

causes.description: The leading urogenital trigger in young sexually active adults. Bacteria can be cultured or detected by PCR in the genitourinary tract and have been demonstrated in joint tissue in chronic cases. Treatment of both patient and sexual partners is mandatory but does not consistently reverse established arthritis.

causes.cause: Bacterial gastroenteritis (Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Yersinia, Clostridioides difficile)
causes.description: Enteric bacterial pathogens trigger reactive arthritis in 1-15% of infected individuals, with rates highest after Yersinia and Shigella. Outbreak settings (cruise ships, restaurants, water contamination) can produce clusters of cases.
Recognized urogenital triggers in addition to Chlamydia. M. genitalium increasingly identified with sensitive PCR; treated with azithromycin or moxifloxacin depending on resistance patterns.

A growing literature documents reactive arthritis after COVID-19, both acute and post-acute. Pathogenesis appears similar to other reactive arthritides; most cases respond to NSAIDs and short-course corticosteroids.

HLA-B27 positivity is the strongest known genetic risk factor, present in 30-80% of patients with reactive arthritis versus 5-10% of the general population. HLA-B27 alone does not cause disease but markedly amplifies risk after a triggering infection.

Post-streptococcal reactive arthritis is distinct from acute rheumatic fever; both can follow group A streptococcal pharyngitis. Reactive arthritis in this context lacks the carditis of rheumatic fever and is treated with NSAIDs.

riskFactors.description: Present in 30-80% of patients versus 5-10% of the general population; strongest genetic risk factor and associated with higher likelihood of chronic axial disease.

riskFactors.factor: Recent gastrointestinal infection (Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Yersinia)
riskFactors.description: Population-based studies show reactive arthritis develops in 1-15% of patients with these gastroenteritides. Outbreak settings amplify case clusters.

riskFactors.factor: Recent urogenital infection (Chlamydia trachomatis, Mycoplasma genitalium)
riskFactors.description: Sexually transmitted infections account for the majority of male reactive arthritis cases. Screening and treatment of sexual partners is essential.
9:1 male predominance in post-venereal reactive arthritis, reflecting C. trachomatis epidemiology and possibly hormonal factors.
Peak incidence in young adults, consistent with peak rates of triggering infections and HLA-B27 expression.
First-degree relatives of patients with ankylosing spondylitis, psoriatic arthritis, or reactive arthritis have increased risk through shared HLA-B27 and other susceptibility alleles.
HIV-positive patients have higher rates of reactive arthritis and more aggressive disease; co-occurrence requires careful immunomodulation strategy.

affectedDemographics: Annual incidence varies widely (0.6-27 per 100,000) depending on background rates of triggering infections.
Post-venereal form: men outnumber women approximately 9:1, age 20-40, HLA-B27 positive in over 70%.
Post-dysenteric form: roughly equal sex distribution; HLA-B27 positive in 30-60%.
Outbreak-related cases: case clusters reported after Yersinia outbreaks in Scandinavia, Shigella in cruise ships, and Salmonella in restaurants worldwide.
Pediatric cases: less common but reported after Salmonella, Yersinia, Campylobacter, and Streptococcus pyogenes infections.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is clinical, supported by serologic, microbiological, and imaging data; there is no single definitive test. The Assessment of SpondyloArthritis International Society (ASAS) classification criteria for peripheral or axial spondyloarthritis are widely used. History focuses on recent infection (4-6 weeks before joint onset): episodes of urethritis, cervicitis, dysuria, or unprotected sexual exposure, and episodes of diarrhea, vomiting, or abdominal pain. Exam includes joint count (asymmetric oligoarthritis of lower limbs), enthesitis (Achilles, plantar fascia, patellar, iliac crest), dactylitis assessment, axial assessment (modified Schober test, lateral spinal flexion, FABER, sacroiliac joint tenderness), and screen for extra-articular features (conjunctivitis, uveitis, keratoderma, balanitis, oral ulcers, nail pitting, psoriasis). Laboratory testing includes complete blood count, ESR and CRP (elevated in active disease), rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (negative in reactive arthritis — important for distinction from rheumatoid arthritis), HLA-B27 typing, and screening for the triggering infection: urine NAAT for Chlamydia trachomatis and Mycoplasma genitalium, stool culture or PCR for Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Yersinia, and C. difficile, ASO/anti-DNase B if streptococcal trigger suspected, and HIV testing. Joint aspiration with synovial fluid analysis and culture excludes septic arthritis and crystal disease — synovial fluid in reactive arthritis is inflammatory (5,000-50,000 WBC/µL, predominantly neutrophils) and sterile. Imaging is selective: plain radiographs are usually normal early; ultrasound and MRI detect enthesitis and synovitis; MRI of sacroiliac joints identifies early sacroiliitis when axial symptoms are present. Differential diagnosis includes septic arthritis (always excluded by aspiration), gout, psoriatic arthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, Lyme arthritis, and viral arthritis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the temporal relationship to triggering infection and the asymmetric lower-limb oligoarthritis, enthesitis, and dactylitis pattern characteristic of reactive arthritis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Detailed history and joint exam by a rheumatologist; 30-45 minute consultation including review of recent infections, sexual history, and systemic features.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents systemic inflammation and tracks treatment response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw; results within hours. ESR and CRP are usually elevated in active disease.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Negative results support reactive arthritis or other seronegative spondyloarthritis; positive results suggest rheumatoid arthritis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw; results within 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Supports diagnosis of spondyloarthritis-spectrum disease and predicts axial disease and chronicity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw; results within 1-2 weeks. Positive in 30-80% of reactive arthritis cases.

diagnosticTests.test: Urine NAAT for Chlamydia trachomatis and Mycoplasma genitalium
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies urogenital triggers for post-venereal cases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Provide a first-catch urine sample; results within 1-3 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Stool culture or multiplex PCR for enteric pathogens
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies enteric triggers (Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Yersinia, C. difficile)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fresh stool sample; PCR results within 1-2 days, culture within 3-7 days. Often negative by the time arthritis develops because the gastroenteritis has resolved.

diagnosticTests.test: Joint aspiration with synovial fluid analysis and culture
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes septic arthritis and crystal arthropathy; synovial fluid in reactive arthritis is inflammatory and sterile
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Aspirated under sterile technique with local anesthetic; 5-10 minutes. WBC count, Gram stain, culture, and crystal analysis available same day to 48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI of sacroiliac joints (when axial symptoms)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects early sacroiliitis (bone marrow edema, erosions) when plain radiographs are normal
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes in the MRI scanner; results within 24 hours. Sensitive for early inflammatory change supporting spondyloarthritis classification.

treatmentOverview: Treatment has three goals: control joint and entheseal inflammation, treat the persistent triggering infection where applicable, and prevent chronic disease. First-line for acute and short-duration disease is high-dose non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (naproxen 500 mg twice daily, indomethacin 50 mg three times daily, or diclofenac 75 mg twice daily) titrated for response and tolerance, with proton pump inhibitor cover in those at gastrointestinal risk. Intra-articular corticosteroid injection delivers rapid relief of involved knees, ankles, and large joints, particularly when limited to 1-2 joints. Systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 20-40 mg daily, taper over 4-8 weeks) are used in polyarticular or refractory disease, in patients with uveitis, or when extra-articular features (severe keratoderma, balanitis) demand systemic control. Antibiotics are mandatory for active urogenital infection (azithromycin 1 g single dose or doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days for Chlamydia trachomatis; combination treatment with rifampicin has been studied for chronic reactive arthritis with limited benefit). Antibiotics do not reliably resolve established arthritis once joints are inflamed, but they prevent reinfection and partner transmission. Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (sulfasalazine 2-3 g/day, methotrexate 15-25 mg weekly) are added in patients with persistent peripheral arthritis beyond 3-6 months despite NSAIDs and corticosteroids. Biologic therapy with TNF inhibitors (adalimumab, etanercept, infliximab) is reserved for patients with persistent peripheral or axial disease who fail conventional DMARDs, mirroring ASAS-EULAR recommendations for spondyloarthritis. IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab) and IL-23 inhibitors are emerging options. Physical therapy maintains range of motion, strength, and posture; ophthalmology manages uveitis with topical or systemic steroids; dermatology supports keratoderma and balanitis care.

medicalTreatments.name: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) — naproxen 500 mg twice daily, indomethacin 50 mg three times daily, or diclofenac 75 mg twice daily
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for acute reactive arthritis. Often combined with proton pump inhibitor gastroprotection. Continued until inflammation has settled, typically 4-12 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom relief in 60-80% of patients with acute oligoarthritis; modestly effective for axial disease

medicalTreatments.name: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone 40 mg or methylprednisolone 40 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Useful for monoarthritis or oligoarthritis with one or two predominantly affected joints. Provides rapid, durable relief. Limited to 2-3 injections per joint per year.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Substantial symptomatic relief in 70-90% of treated joints, lasting 6-12 weeks or longer

medicalTreatments.name: Oral prednisone 20-40 mg daily, tapered over 4-8 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Used for polyarticular or refractory disease, severe enthesitis, or extra-articular features. Tapered to minimum effective dose with overlap by DMARD when needed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom improvement in 70-90% of patients; useful bridge to DMARD therapy

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotics for active urogenital infection (azithromycin 1 g single dose or doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Mandatory for active C. trachomatis or M. genitalium infection; treat sexual partners. Does not reliably reverse established arthritis but prevents reinfection.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Eradicates active infection in 90%+ of patients; arthritis outcome variable

medicalTreatments.name: Sulfasalazine 2-3 g/day
medicalTreatments.description: Conventional DMARD with most evidence in reactive arthritis among the spondyloarthritides; particularly useful in peripheral disease persisting beyond 3-6 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest benefit in peripheral arthritis; less effective for axial disease

medicalTreatments.name: Methotrexate 15-25 mg weekly with folic acid
medicalTreatments.description: Used in persistent peripheral arthritis as an alternative or addition to sulfasalazine. Monitor liver function and complete blood count.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom improvement in 50-70% of patients with persistent peripheral disease

medicalTreatments.name: TNF inhibitors (adalimumab 40 mg every 2 weeks, etanercept 50 mg weekly, infliximab 5 mg/kg IV)
medicalTreatments.description: Biologic therapy for patients with persistent peripheral or axial disease despite conventional DMARDs and NSAIDs. Mirrors ASAS-EULAR recommendations for spondyloarthritis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Major response in 60-70% of patients with persistent spondyloarthritis-spectrum disease

medicalTreatments.name: IL-17 inhibitor (secukinumab 150-300 mg subcutaneously)
medicalTreatments.description: Emerging option for axial and peripheral spondyloarthritis; particularly useful when TNF inhibitors are contraindicated or ineffective.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ASAS40 response in 30-50% of patients with axial spondyloarthritis at 16 weeks

medicalTreatments.name: Topical and systemic uveitis therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Topical prednisolone acetate 1% drops with cycloplegic for anterior uveitis; oral or periocular steroids for severe disease; managed by ophthalmology.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolution of acute uveitis in 70-90% of episodes; ongoing monitoring required for recurrence

surgicalOptions.name: Synovectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of inflamed synovium reserved for persistent monoarthritis refractory to medical therapy. Rare in modern era of biologics.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic improvement in 60-80% of selected cases; long-term outcomes depend on systemic disease control

surgicalOptions.name: Joint replacement (knee, hip)
surgicalOptions.description: Definitive treatment for end-stage joint destruction from chronic reactive arthritis. Uncommon outcome in the modern treatment era.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Comparable to other inflammatory arthritis cohorts; 10-year implant survival 85-95%

alternativeTreatments.name: Physical therapy and exercise program
alternativeTreatments.description: Maintains range of motion, strength, and posture; particularly important in axial disease. Combined aerobic, flexibility, and strengthening exercises with periodic specialist review.

alternativeTreatments.name: Education and self-management programs
alternativeTreatments.description: Patient-centered education on disease course, infection prevention, and adherence improves long-term outcomes; offered through spondyloarthritis support groups and rheumatology services.

whySeeSpecialist: All patients with suspected reactive arthritis benefit from rheumatology evaluation to confirm diagnosis, exclude septic arthritis and other causes of inflammatory arthritis, and plan staged treatment. Co-management with infectious diseases (for triggering infection), ophthalmology (for uveitis), and dermatology (for keratoderma and balanitis) is standard.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a rheumatologist with experience in spondyloarthritis spectrum disease, ideally affiliated with a center capable of HLA-B27 testing, joint imaging including MRI of sacroiliac joints, and biologic therapy management. For sexually transmitted infection triggers, ensure infectious disease or sexual health partnership for partner notification and treatment.
hospitalCriteria: Access to HLA-B27 typing and MRI of sacroiliac joints
Joint aspiration capability for synovial fluid analysis
Pharmacy access to TNF inhibitors and IL-17 inhibitors
keyFacilities: Sexual health clinics for partner notification
preventionStrategies: Practice safer sex with consistent condom use and prompt treatment of sexually transmitted infections to reduce Chlamydia trachomatis and Mycoplasma genitalium triggers.
Follow food safety practices: cook meat thoroughly, avoid unpasteurized dairy, wash fresh produce, and refrigerate leftovers promptly to reduce Salmonella, Shigella, and Campylobacter exposure.
Maintain hand hygiene during travel and in outbreak settings.
Treat enteric infections promptly when severe and seek follow-up if joint symptoms develop within weeks.
Avoid antibiotic-driven C. difficile by minimizing unnecessary antibiotic exposure.
Manage HIV and other immunocompromising conditions to reduce severity of reactive arthritis when it occurs.
lifestyleModifications: Adhere to prescribed medication including DMARDs and biologics; do not stop suddenly.
Complete the full antibiotic course for triggering infection and ensure sexual partners are treated.
Maintain regular low-impact exercise (swimming, cycling, walking) during recovery to preserve range of motion.
Use heat or cold therapy on affected joints as adjunctive symptom management.
Stop smoking; tobacco reduces NSAID efficacy and increases cardiovascular risk in chronic inflammatory disease.
Avoid heavy alcohol use, which interacts with NSAIDs, methotrexate, and sulfasalazine.
Attend regular rheumatology follow-up to track disease activity and adjust therapy.

recommended: Mediterranean dietary pattern (vegetables, fruits, legumes, whole grains, olive oil, fish) with anti-inflammatory potential
Omega-3-rich foods (oily fish, flaxseed, walnuts) for joint and cardiovascular health
Adequate calcium and vitamin D in patients on corticosteroids to protect bone
Adequate protein 1.0-1.2 g/kg/day during active inflammation to preserve muscle
avoid: Excess alcohol that interacts with NSAIDs and DMARDs
Ultra-processed foods with added sugar that may amplify systemic inflammation
Folate-poor diets while on methotrexate (folic acid supplementation given)
Raw or undercooked seafood and meat during heavy immunosuppression

exerciseGuidelines: Low-impact aerobic activity (swimming, cycling, elliptical) 150 minutes per week preserves cardiovascular fitness without aggravating inflamed joints. Range-of-motion and stretching exercises performed daily, particularly for the spine and lower limbs in axial disease. Strength training is reintroduced gradually as inflammation settles. Physical therapy guidance optimizes program design in axial spondyloarthritis.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed NSAIDs, DMARDs, or biologics on schedule with food where required.
Use cold packs after activity for inflamed joints; warm baths for general stiffness.
Stretch the spine, hips, and lower limbs daily, particularly mornings.
Track joint symptoms in a simple diary to inform rheumatology visits.
Practice safer sex and complete antibiotic regimens if a sexually transmitted infection was the trigger.
Monitor for signs of uveitis (eye pain, redness, photophobia) and seek immediate ophthalmology review if they occur.
prognosis: Prognosis is generally favorable. Approximately 50-70% of patients achieve complete remission within 6-12 months of disease onset, particularly those with post-dysenteric reactive arthritis and HLA-B27-negative disease. The remaining 15-30% develop chronic disease that may persist for years or evolve into formal axial or peripheral spondyloarthritis. HLA-B27 positivity, severity at presentation, axial involvement, family history of spondyloarthritis, and persistent infection (especially Chlamydia) predict chronicity. Modern treatment with NSAIDs, DMARDs, and TNF or IL-17 inhibitors has substantially improved outcomes, with most patients achieving sustained low disease activity. Recurrence after resolution can occur with re-exposure to triggering infection. Long-term complications include chronic axial disease with reduced spinal mobility, joint damage from persistent peripheral arthritis, recurrent uveitis, and rare cardiovascular complications (aortitis, aortic regurgitation, conduction system disease) in long-standing disease.
recoveryTimeline: Acute reactive arthritis: peripheral joint inflammation improves substantially within 4-12 weeks of NSAID therapy; full resolution typically within 3-12 months. Enthesitis and dactylitis may take longer (4-6 months) to fully settle. Axial disease often persists longer and may require ongoing therapy. Chronic disease (>6 months) requires sustained immunomodulatory care indefinitely.
complications: Chronic persistent arthritis in 15-30% of patients, sometimes evolving to formal axial or peripheral spondyloarthritis indistinguishable from ankylosing spondylitis.
Recurrent anterior uveitis requiring ongoing ophthalmology follow-up; episodes can cause permanent visual loss if untreated.
Joint damage and reduced range of motion in untreated chronic disease.
Cardiac involvement (aortitis, aortic regurgitation, conduction system disease) in long-standing reactive arthritis (rare but recognized).
Recurrence after disease resolution with re-exposure to the triggering infection.
Adverse effects of long-term NSAIDs (gastrointestinal, renal, cardiovascular) and immunosuppression (infection risk).

supportResources.name: Spondylitis Association of America (SAA)
supportResources.description: Patient advocacy organization for spondyloarthritis spectrum diseases including reactive arthritis.

supportResources.name: American College of Rheumatology — Reactive Arthritis
supportResources.description: Patient education from the American College of Rheumatology with current treatment information.

supportResources.name: National Axial Spondyloarthritis Society (NASS)
supportResources.description: UK patient society with educational resources, exercise guides, and peer support for spondyloarthritis.

supportResources.name: Arthritis Foundation
supportResources.description: US patient organization with disease education and resources for living with inflammatory arthritis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rheumatoid arthritis is a symmetric polyarthritis predominantly affecting small joints of the hands and feet (MCPs, PIPs, wrists, MTPs) with positive rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies in 70-80%. Reactive arthritis is asymmetric, lower-limb predominant, RF and anti-CCP negative, with enthesitis, dactylitis, and HLA-B27 association.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gout
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gout produces sudden severe monoarthritis classically of the first metatarsophalangeal joint with elevated serum urate and monosodium urate crystals on synovial fluid analysis. Reactive arthritis follows infection by 1-4 weeks, often involves multiple lower-limb joints, lacks crystals, and shows synovial inflammation without urate.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriatic Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Psoriatic arthritis is closely related (same family of spondyloarthritides) but requires psoriatic skin or nail disease and shows distinct patterns including DIP joint involvement and arthritis mutilans. Reactive arthritis follows recent infection rather than psoriasis; both share HLA-B27 and enthesitis features.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ankylosing Spondylitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ankylosing spondylitis is a chronic axial spondyloarthritis with insidious onset of inflammatory back pain, sacroiliitis, and HLA-B27 positivity (over 90%) without preceding infection. Reactive arthritis has acute onset 1-4 weeks after infection and is more peripheral-joint predominant, though long-standing disease can resemble AS.

confusedWithConditions.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lupus is a multisystem autoimmune disease with photosensitive rash, oral ulcers, serositis, kidney and CNS involvement, positive ANA and dsDNA, and low complement. Reactive arthritis lacks lupus serology and systemic features and follows recent infection.

relatedConditions.name: Psoriatic Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Closely related spondyloarthritis sharing enthesitis, dactylitis, and HLA-B27 features; sometimes difficult to distinguish in patients with overlapping skin disease.

relatedConditions.name: Ankylosing Spondylitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic reactive arthritis can evolve into axial spondyloarthritis indistinguishable from ankylosing spondylitis.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Main differential among inflammatory arthritides; distinguished by symmetry, joint distribution, and serology.

relatedConditions.name: Gout
relatedConditions.relevance: Important differential for acute monoarthritis; crystal analysis distinguishes.

relatedConditions.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
relatedConditions.relevance: Multisystem autoimmune differential requiring serologic distinction.

relatedConditions.name: Gonorrhea
relatedConditions.relevance: Gonococcal arthritis is an important septic differential; reactive arthritis is sterile and follows by weeks rather than during acute infection.

faqs.question: What is reactive arthritis?
faqs.answer: Reactive arthritis is a sterile inflammatory joint disease that develops 1-4 weeks after a urogenital or gastrointestinal infection in a genetically susceptible host. It typically causes asymmetric joint pain and swelling in the lower limbs, often with enthesitis, eye inflammation, and skin lesions.

faqs.question: What causes reactive arthritis?
faqs.answer: Reactive arthritis is triggered by infections elsewhere in the body. Common triggers include Chlamydia trachomatis (sexually transmitted), and Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, or Yersinia (gastrointestinal). SARS-CoV-2 and streptococcal infection are also recognized triggers. HLA-B27 positivity strongly amplifies susceptibility.

faqs.question: Is reactive arthritis the same as Reiter's syndrome?
faqs.answer: Reactive arthritis is the modern term for what was previously called Reiter's syndrome. The historical triad of arthritis, conjunctivitis, and urethritis is present in only 30-50% of patients. The name was changed because Hans Reiter participated in Nazi-era medical experimentation, and reactive arthritis better describes the underlying mechanism.

faqs.question: How is reactive arthritis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical, based on the temporal relationship to triggering infection, the typical asymmetric lower-limb oligoarthritis with enthesitis or dactylitis, and supportive testing: elevated ESR/CRP, negative rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP, HLA-B27 typing, and identification of the triggering infection by urine NAAT or stool PCR.

faqs.question: Is reactive arthritis curable?
faqs.answer: Approximately 50-70% of patients achieve complete remission within 6-12 months. 15-30% develop chronic disease that may persist for years or evolve into formal spondyloarthritis. HLA-B27 positivity, axial involvement, and persistent Chlamydia infection predict chronicity. Modern treatments achieve sustained low disease activity in most chronic cases.

faqs.question: What is the first-line treatment for reactive arthritis?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment is high-dose NSAIDs (naproxen 500 mg twice daily, indomethacin 50 mg three times daily, or diclofenac 75 mg twice daily) with proton pump inhibitor cover. Intra-articular corticosteroid injection is added for affected large joints. Oral prednisone is used for polyarticular or refractory disease.

faqs.question: Do antibiotics cure reactive arthritis?
faqs.answer: Antibiotics treat the underlying infection but do not reliably reverse established arthritis. They are mandatory for active urogenital infection (azithromycin or doxycycline for Chlamydia) to prevent reinfection and partner transmission. Studies of long-course combination antibiotics in chronic reactive arthritis show limited benefit.

faqs.question: Is reactive arthritis contagious?
faqs.answer: Reactive arthritis itself is not contagious. The triggering infections (Chlamydia, Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Yersinia) are transmissible, and partners or contacts may benefit from screening and treatment, but the joint disease is an immune response and is not directly transmitted person to person.

faqs.question: What does HLA-B27 mean for reactive arthritis?
faqs.answer: HLA-B27 is a genetic marker present in 30-80% of reactive arthritis patients versus 5-10% of the general population. It strongly amplifies susceptibility after a triggering infection and is associated with more severe and chronic disease, axial involvement, and higher recurrence rates. HLA-B27 alone does not cause disease.

faqs.question: What is dactylitis?
faqs.answer: Dactylitis (sausage digit) is diffuse swelling of an entire finger or toe involving joints, tendons, and entheses. It is a classic feature of spondyloarthritis including reactive arthritis and psoriatic arthritis. Unlike isolated joint swelling, dactylitis produces uniform fusiform enlargement of the digit.

faqs.question: Can reactive arthritis come back?
faqs.answer: Yes. Recurrence rates of 10-30% over years are reported, particularly with re-exposure to the triggering infection. HLA-B27-positive patients have higher recurrence risk. Safer sex practices, food safety, and prompt treatment of infections reduce risk.

faqs.question: Can reactive arthritis affect the eyes?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis is common during the acute phase. More serious anterior uveitis can occur with eye redness, pain, photophobia, and reduced vision; it requires urgent ophthalmology evaluation. Recurrent uveitis is associated with HLA-B27 positivity.

faqs.question: Can reactive arthritis follow COVID-19?
faqs.answer: Yes. Reactive arthritis following SARS-CoV-2 infection is recognized in published case series since 2020. Typical asymmetric lower-limb oligoarthritis develops weeks after acute COVID-19. Most cases respond to NSAIDs and short-course corticosteroids; some persist and require DMARD therapy.

faqs.question: What is enthesitis?
faqs.answer: Enthesitis is inflammation at sites where tendons or ligaments attach to bone. In reactive arthritis common sites include the Achilles tendon, plantar fascia, patellar tendon, and iliac crest. It is a classic spondyloarthritis feature and can be disabling. Diagnosis is clinical and supported by ultrasound or MRI.

faqs.question: Can children get reactive arthritis?
faqs.answer: Yes, though less common than in adults. Pediatric cases have been reported after Salmonella, Yersinia, Campylobacter, and Streptococcus pyogenes infections. Presentation, evaluation, and treatment principles mirror adult disease, with NSAIDs and selective corticosteroid use.

faqs.question: Are skin lesions a sign of reactive arthritis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Keratoderma blennorrhagicum (hyperkeratotic plaques on soles, palms, and scalp) and circinate balanitis (serpiginous lesions on the glans) are classic features in some patients with reactive arthritis. They can be confused with pustular psoriasis and require dermatology input.

faqs.question: Can reactive arthritis cause back pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Sacroiliitis and spondylitis can develop in reactive arthritis, particularly in HLA-B27-positive patients. Inflammatory back pain (worse at rest, better with movement, with morning stiffness over 30 minutes) is the classic presentation. MRI of the sacroiliac joints helps confirm the diagnosis when radiographs are normal.

faqs.question: When are biologics used for reactive arthritis?
faqs.answer: TNF inhibitors (adalimumab, etanercept, infliximab) and IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab) are reserved for patients with persistent peripheral or axial disease who fail NSAIDs and conventional DMARDs such as sulfasalazine or methotrexate. They mirror ASAS-EULAR recommendations for spondyloarthritis spectrum diseases.

faqs.question: Can sexually transmitted infections cause arthritis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chlamydia trachomatis is the leading sexually transmitted trigger of reactive arthritis in young adults; Mycoplasma genitalium is another recognized trigger. Treatment includes appropriate antibiotics for the patient and sexual partners, alongside management of the arthritis itself.

faqs.question: How is reactive arthritis different from septic arthritis?
faqs.answer: Septic arthritis involves direct joint infection with positive synovial fluid culture, severe single-joint pain with high fever, and requires antibiotics and drainage. Reactive arthritis is sterile with no organism in the joint, follows infection elsewhere by 1-4 weeks, and is managed with anti-inflammatories and immunomodulation. Aspiration is mandatory in monoarthritis to distinguish.

faqs.question: Can I exercise with reactive arthritis?
faqs.answer: Low-impact exercise (swimming, cycling, walking) is encouraged once acute inflammation has settled. It preserves range of motion, prevents stiffness, and supports cardiovascular health. Physical therapy guidance during the active phase and structured exercise during remission improve long-term function.
